title: The four properties of a good algorithm — finite, definite, input, output

detail.first_line: # The four properties of a good algorithm — finite, definite, input, output

detail.note: Word count updated from 1410 to 1501 after lab-activity block appended to In Practice. Still within [1200, 1600]; 7.5 min at 200 wpm and 6.5 min at 230 wpm both within [6, 8].

Both topic references point to earlier sequence entries. 'server' appears once (line 140) as an everyday word in a real-world failure example, not introduced as a technical concept. No forward references detected.

sample.assertion: a computer following them could loop forever, behave unpredictably, or produce no result
When you follow a recipe, you do not stir indefinitely; the recipe says 'stir for 2 minutes'
An infinite loop — a situation where the algorithm never terminates
Add some salt is not definite. Add half a teaspoon of salt is definite.
The number of inputs can be zero or more. Zero inputs is a legitimate answer.
A sorting algorithm does not output a random number — it outputs the sorted version of the list it received
ATM cash dispensing, search engine, airport boarding examples
payment system that loops indefinitely when a server is slow is a finiteness failure; a form that accepts 'any name' is a definiteness failure
Lab activity: write everyday process as steps, apply checklist, partner-swap for definiteness gaps

detail.note: All four properties introduced with bold-term + plain definition on first use: 'finiteness' (line 16), 'definiteness' (line 34), 'infinite loop' (line 20), 'termination' (line 20), 'well-defined algorithm' (line 82). Concrete analogies precede abstractions throughout. Short sentences, active voice, direct 'you' address consistent. No hedging language found.

All sections use tables, numbered/bulleted lists, and short paragraphs. In Practice checklist (lines 129-133) rendered as numbered list. Lab activity (lines 151-153) rendered as numbered list. Definitions consistently follow bold-term / plain-meaning pattern.

Reading is entirely bounded by the four-property checklist. Real-world examples (ATM, search engine, airport) are applications of the four properties, not new concepts introduced. Lab activity is a direct checklist-application exercise. 'server' used informally once (line 140) in a failure-pattern example — not taught as a concept. No material beyond the topic headline detected.

gold_example_comparison.note: No M1-week-1 reading gold example at .claude/references/gold-examples/reading/. Evaluated against written standards in artifact-mix.json. Reading meets all written standards.

verdict: PASS — all 8 checks clean (0 hard fails, 0 soft findings); 1501 words within [1200, 1600] standard-profile range, 7.5 min at 200 wpm within [6, 8]; all three resources cited with matching bibliography; no forward references; 10/10 sampled assertions trace to corpus including the new lab-activity block (corpus §9); beginner readability and formatting standards met throughout; no scope creep; no assessment references.
